NVDA.title: SCHD ETF Stock C&H Pattern Points To A Rebound As Inflows Hit $8.2B

MU.title: Memory Boom Fuels DRAM ETF's Sprint To $10 Billion, But Risks Remain

macro_events.event_summary: A Yahoo Finance piece highlights that ~$800B in AI capex is propping up GDP and equity markets while real wages decline and consumers cut discretionary goods spending — framing a K-shaped economy split between AI infrastructure and the consumer.
primary_drivers: AI capex concentration boosting hyperscaler/semiconductor revenue
Falling real wages compressing low/middle-income discretionary spend
Bifurcation between enterprise AI demand and consumer goods demand

beneficiaries.rationale: Direct primary recipient of the $800B AI capex cycle — dominant GPU share in hyperscaler training/inference buildouts. The story explicitly frames AI spending as the GDP driver, which is essentially NVDA's revenue line.
Vertiv is a pure-play data center power/cooling supplier; AI capex magnitude headlines reinforce backlog visibility. Less crowded than NVDA so reacts to narrative reinforcement.
Data center REIT with direct leasing exposure to hyperscaler AI buildout; $800B capex framing supports continued tight vacancy and pricing power thesis.
AI server revenue is the explicit beneficiary of hyperscaler/enterprise AI capex; ISG backlog tied directly to the spending wave the article describes.

losers.rationale: Dollar Tree's core customer is the low-income consumer cited as cutting back on goods amid falling real wages — direct revenue exposure to the weak side of the K.
Dollar General serves the same low-income demographic; same-store sales already pressured by exactly the real-wage compression the article highlights.
Target's discretionary goods mix (apparel, home) is more exposed than staples-heavy peers; consumer pullback on goods directly hits comps.
Toys/discretionary goods squarely in the 'consumers cutting back on goods' bucket; weak real wages compress household discretionary budgets.
Whirlpool sells big-ticket discretionary durable goods to a wage-pressured consumer; volumes already weak and narrative reinforces downside.

headlines: $800B in AI spending is juicing GDP and stocks — while real wages fall and Americans cut back on goods
